items.title: Visual DAG Workflow Builder
items.description: A drag-and-drop canvas to chain bots and agents into multi-step pipelines with conditional branches and parallel execution paths. Solves the pain of manually wiring bots together via prompts — teams can visually compose 'PR review → security scan → changelog update' into a single reusable workflow.

items.title: Execution Replay & Re-run
items.description: Replay any past execution with the exact same prompt, context, and inputs — or modify just the prompt and re-run. Solves the pain of debugging bot failures where you need to reproduce the exact conditions without waiting for the next webhook event.

items.title: Prompt Version History
items.description: Track every change to bot prompt templates with diffs, authored timestamps, and one-click rollback. Teams break prompt templates and have no way to recover the last working version — this adds Git-like history to prompts without requiring any Git knowledge.

items.title: Multi-Provider Fallback Chains
items.description: Configure ordered fallback chains (e.g., Claude → OpenCode → Gemini) per bot so that if one provider fails or rate-limits, execution automatically retries on the next. Eliminates the single point of failure that causes automation blackouts during provider outages.

items.title: Bot Template Marketplace
items.description: A curated gallery of pre-built bot templates (PR reviewer, release note generator, dependency auditor, standup summarizer) that teams can install with one click and customize. Dramatically reduces time-to-value for new teams who don't know what automations to start with.

items.title: AI Cost & Token Usage Dashboard
items.description: Track token consumption, estimated API cost, and execution time per bot, team, and project over time with trend charts. Engineering managers have no visibility into AI spend — this surfaces cost attribution before bills arrive.

items.title: Slack Execution Notifications
items.description: Route bot execution results, failures, and summaries to configurable Slack channels with message templates. Teams miss critical failures because they have to actively check the dashboard — push notifications eliminate the monitoring burden.

items.title: Human-in-the-Loop Approval Gates
items.description: Add optional approval steps to workflows where a designated team member must approve the AI output before it's posted, merged, or acted upon. Addresses the trust problem — teams want AI automation but not fully autonomous actions on production systems.

items.title: In-Platform Prompt Playground
items.description: Test and iterate on bot prompts directly in the dashboard with a live preview panel, sample inputs, and output history — without triggering a real execution. Engineers waste time deploying prompt changes just to see if they work; this tightens the iteration loop dramatically.

items.title: Execution Digest Reports
items.description: Daily or weekly email/Slack digests summarizing all bot executions: what ran, what succeeded, what failed, and cost summaries. Managers currently have no async way to stay informed — digests deliver situational awareness without requiring dashboard visits.

items.title: Secrets & Credential Vault
items.description: A built-in encrypted secrets store for API keys, tokens, and environment variables that bots can reference by name in prompts and CLI configs. Teams currently hardcode credentials or manage them out-of-band — centralizing secrets improves security and reduces onboarding friction.

items.title: Conditional Trigger Rules Engine
items.description: Add filter conditions to triggers (e.g., 'only run PR Review bot if PR has >200 lines changed' or 'only on files matching src/**') using a simple rule builder. Bots run too broadly today, wasting tokens and adding noise — conditions let teams target automation precisely.

items.title: Execution Log Annotations
items.description: Allow engineers to annotate specific log lines with notes, flag them as bugs or insights, and link to follow-up issues. Execution logs are ephemeral and hard to mine — annotations turn raw output into institutional knowledge.

items.title: Agent Quality Scoring
items.description: Collect thumbs-up/thumbs-down feedback on bot outputs and surface per-agent quality scores over time with trend analysis. Teams have no quantitative way to know if their bots are getting better or worse as they iterate on prompts.

items.title: Issue Tracker Integration (Jira / Linear)
items.description: Create triggers from Jira/Linear issue events (new bug, status change, label added) and allow bots to write back comments or update issue fields. Extends automation beyond GitHub to the full engineering workflow where work is actually tracked.

items.title: Execution Rate Limits & Quotas
items.description: Set per-bot, per-team, or per-project daily/hourly execution caps with alerting when approaching limits. Without guardrails, a misconfigured trigger can run a bot thousands of times and blow through API budgets overnight.

items.title: Team Activity Feed
items.description: A real-time chronological feed showing all bot runs, configuration changes, and team member actions across a project — like a GitHub activity timeline but for automation. Removes the 'who ran what and when' mystery that slows incident response.

items.title: One-Click Bot Cloning
items.description: Clone any existing bot (including its triggers, prompt template, and settings) as a starting point for a new one. Teams repeatedly build similar bots from scratch — cloning compresses the creation of variant automations from hours to seconds.

items.title: Context File Attachments for Bots
items.description: Attach reference files (coding standards, architecture docs, runbooks) to a bot so the AI always has project-specific context injected into its prompt. Without this, teams bloat prompts with static context or get generic AI outputs that ignore their conventions.

items.title: Execution Dependency Graph
items.description: Visualize which bots triggered other bots or workflows in a given execution chain, with timing and status overlaid. Complex pipelines become black boxes — the graph makes causal relationships and bottlenecks immediately visible.

items.title: Role-Based Access Control (RBAC)
items.description: Fine-grained permissions so team admins can restrict who can create bots, trigger executions, view logs, or manage credentials. As teams grow, unrestricted access becomes a liability — RBAC aligns the platform with enterprise security requirements.

items.title: GitHub App Installation Flow
items.description: Replace manual webhook setup with a first-class GitHub App installation that auto-configures repositories, permissions, and event subscriptions. Manual webhook setup is the #1 onboarding drop-off point — removing it gets teams to first automation in minutes.

items.title: Structured Output Action Blocks
items.description: Let bots emit structured action blocks (create PR comment, open GitHub issue, post Slack message, update file) that the platform executes automatically — rather than the bot doing it via CLI. Separates AI reasoning from side-effect execution, making outputs auditable and reversible.

items.title: Dry-Run Mode
items.description: Execute a bot against real inputs but suppress all external side effects (no PR comments posted, no files changed) and display what would have happened. Teams fear running new bots in production — dry-run eliminates the risk of first execution.

items.title: AI-Powered Trigger Suggestions
items.description: Analyze a repository's GitHub events and activity patterns to suggest relevant trigger configurations and starter prompt templates. New users stare at a blank configuration screen — contextual suggestions reduce time-to-first-bot from days to minutes.

items.title: Full-Text Execution Log Search
items.description: Search across all historical execution logs by keyword, bot name, date range, or status. Currently logs are only viewable per-execution — search transforms the execution history into a queryable knowledge base for debugging and auditing.

items.title: Incoming Webhook Inspector
items.description: Capture and display raw incoming webhook payloads in a real-time inspector panel, showing which bots matched, which were skipped, and why. Debugging trigger mismatches is currently impossible without external tooling — the inspector makes it self-service.

items.title: Programmatic API Keys
items.description: Generate scoped API keys so teams can trigger bots and query execution results from CI pipelines, scripts, or other tools. The platform is currently web-only — API keys unlock it as a programmable automation primitive embedded in any workflow.

items.title: Auto-Generated Changelogs
items.description: A dedicated bot template that aggregates merged PRs, AI summaries, and linked issues into a formatted CHANGELOG.md on each release tag. Writing changelogs is universally hated and perpetually delayed — this eliminates the task entirely.

items.title: Guided Onboarding Wizard
items.description: A step-by-step first-run experience that walks new teams through connecting a repo, choosing a starter bot, and firing their first test execution — with inline guidance at each step. Blank-slate onboarding is the biggest conversion killer; this removes every ambiguity.

items.title: execution_service.py is 748 lines of mixed concerns
items.description: backend/app/services/execution_service.py (748 lines): Combines trigger dispatching, command building, subprocess orchestration, rate-limit tracking, and log streaming in a single class. Extract subprocess management and log streaming into separate modules.

items.title: workflow_execution_service.py is 857 lines
items.description: backend/app/services/workflow_execution_service.py (857 lines): Manages DAG execution, node states, retries, and error modes with complex in-memory threading. Extremely difficult to test individual node execution or retry logic in isolation.

items.title: team_execution_service.py is 764 lines
items.description: backend/app/services/team_execution_service.py (764 lines): Mixed concerns — team routing, message passing, in-memory state, and subprocess orchestration. Locks at lines 33-35 guard shared state accessed in complex call chains.

items.title: teams.py route file is 681 lines
items.description: backend/app/routes/teams.py (681 lines): Single route file handles team CRUD, topology generation, member management, and execution triggers. Should be split into focused sub-blueprints (e.g., teams_crud, teams_execution, teams_topology).

items.title: super_agents.py route file is 628 lines
items.description: backend/app/routes/super_agents.py (628 lines): Handles super agents, documents, sessions, and message routing in one file. Each concern should have its own route file.

items.title: migrations.py is 3102 lines and hard to navigate
items.description: backend/app/db/migrations.py (3102 lines): Entire migration history in a single file. While append-only is intentional, grouping migrations by phase or domain (e.g., migrations_v1.py, migrations_v2.py) would improve discoverability and reduce diff noise.

items.title: Frontend components have near-zero unit test coverage
items.description: frontend/src/components/ and frontend/src/views/: 182 Vue components exist but test coverage is minimal. Only ~10 test files were found. Core views like execution flows, agent management, and workflow editors have no unit tests.

items.title: No integration tests for multi-service workflows
items.description: backend/tests/: 49 test files present but appear to be unit tests only. No integration tests covering the full execution pipeline — trigger → dispatch → subprocess → log streaming → status update.

items.title: print() used instead of logger throughout services
items.description: backend/app/services/execution_service.py lines 167, 233, 241-243, 256, 285, 336-337 and github_service.py lines 76, 93, 96, 100, 104, 108, 114 use print(). These bypass logging config and won't appear in production log aggregation.

items.title: No tests for ExecutionService error paths
items.description: backend/tests/ has no coverage for run_trigger() GitHub clone failures, prompt substitution edge cases, subprocess failure handling, or rate-limit detection in stderr parsing. These are the most critical paths.

items.title: No tests for concurrent/threaded operations
items.description: Services using `_lock` threading (execution_service.py, execution_log_service.py, process_manager.py) have no concurrency tests. Threading bugs only surface under load.

items.title: No tests for budget enforcement boundaries
items.description: Budget enforcement logic in orchestration_service.py has no tests for boundary conditions (exactly at limit, just over, negative balance). Critical for billing correctness.

items.title: Unresolved TODO/FIXME markers in production code
items.description: Multiple service files contain TODO/FIXME comments representing deferred work. Run `grep -rn 'TODO\|FIXME' backend/app/` to enumerate them all and prioritize resolution.

items.title: 40%+ of API route files have no tests
items.description: No test files exist for: backend/app/routes/agents.py, routes/backends.py, routes/plugins.py, routes/executions.py, routes/workflows.py, routes/grd.py, and ~20 other route files. API changes risk undetected regressions across core functionality.

items.title: No tests for App.vue (main layout/state)
items.description: frontend/src/App.vue manages global toast notifications, health polling, sidebar loading, and navigation state — but has zero test coverage. Changes to this file risk breaking the entire application shell silently.

items.title: Type safety bypassed with `any` casts in tests
items.description: frontend/src/webmcp/__tests__/search-sort-cycle.test.ts:81,88,93,100,111,121,131,135 uses multiple `as any` casts. frontend/src/webmcp/useWebMcpPageTools.ts:27,37,43,67,127 uses `Record<string, unknown>` loosely. Tests won't catch type-related regressions.

items.title: Deprecated fields with no removal timeline
items.description: backend/app/services/trigger_service.py:66 retains a deprecated field for backward compatibility with no comment about when it will be removed. These fields accumulate over time and complicate the data model.

items.title: MonitoringSection.vue (1,202 lines) has no unit tests
items.description: frontend/src/components/monitoring/MonitoringSection.vue is a large, complex monitoring dashboard component with no corresponding test file. Changes to monitoring logic, chart rendering, or panel state cannot be validated automatically.

items.title: Scheduler init function spans 330+ lines
items.description: backend/app/services/agent_scheduler_service.py's init function is ~330 lines. Scheduler initialization phases (DB connection, job registration, trigger loading, cron parsing) are all intermixed. This is a coverage and maintainability concern.

items.title: print() used instead of logger across multiple services
items.description: backend/app/services/execution_service.py, github_service.py, and other services use print() for operational messages (26+ instances). Production deployments capture structured logs but not stdout, making these messages invisible in any real deployment.

items.title: plugin_sync_service.py has no test file
items.description: backend/app/services/plugin_sync_service.py has no corresponding test file. Plugin sync is a core feature (syncing skills/hooks/commands from filesystem into DB) with no automated coverage.

items.title: worktree_service.py has no test file
items.description: backend/app/services/worktree_service.py has no corresponding test file. Worktree operations involve git subprocess calls and filesystem mutations — high-risk code without any test coverage.

items.title: rate_limit_service.py has no test file
items.description: backend/app/services/rate_limit_service.py has no dedicated test file. Rate limit logic is critical for preventing overuse of expensive API calls and directly affects system stability.

items.title: grd_sync_service.py has no test file
items.description: backend/app/services/grd_sync_service.py has no dedicated test file despite being responsible for syncing GRD planning state — a complex multi-step operation with side effects.

items.title: audit_log_service.py (new, untracked) has no tests
items.description: backend/app/services/audit_log_service.py is currently untracked (shown in git status as ??). It has no test file. New services should ship with tests before being integrated.

items.title: Vue app has no global error boundary or error reporting
items.description: frontend/src/App.vue and main.ts do not configure a Vue errorHandler or any error boundary component. Uncaught component errors in child views will silently fail or crash the entire SPA with only a browser console error.

items.title: Frontend API calls not cancelled on component unmount
items.description: In frontend/src/services/api.ts, fetch calls do not use AbortController. When a user navigates away mid-request, the request continues and may update stale component state or cause 'component unmounted' Vue warnings.

items.title: model_discovery_service.py has no tests
items.description: backend/app/services/model_discovery_service.py (826 lines) has no corresponding test file. This service handles model enumeration for claude/opencode/gemini and is a critical path. Add tests covering discovery, caching, and failure fallback behavior.

items.title: cliproxy_manager.py has no tests
items.description: backend/app/services/cliproxy_manager.py (812 lines) has no test coverage. Covers process lifecycle, config management, and health checks. Add unit tests with mocked subprocess calls for start/stop/health-check flows.

items.title: Major Vue composables have no tests
items.description: frontend/src/composables/useTeamCanvas.ts (576 lines), useAiChat.ts (427 lines), and useConversation.ts (352 lines) have no test files. These are core UI logic units; add Vitest tests covering state transitions and SSE handling.

items.title: backend_service.py and workflow_trigger_service.py have no tests
items.description: backend/app/services/backend_service.py and backend/app/services/workflow_trigger_service.py lack test coverage. backend_service.py handles account creation and proxy lifecycle; missing tests means regressions go undetected.

items.title: No tests for orchestration, rotation, scheduling, and budget services
items.description: backend/tests/ — orchestration_service.py, rotation_service.py, agent_scheduler_service.py, budget_service.py, and github_service.py have zero test coverage despite handling core platform features.

items.title: Test fixture silently swallows import errors
items.description: backend/tests/conftest.py:62-76 — reset_github_webhook_rate_limit() uses bare except-pass blocks that silently swallow import errors, making test setup failures invisible and hard to debug.

items.title: Widespread use of print() instead of structured logging
items.description: backend/app/services/execution_service.py:79,114,139,150,174,323,389,397,412 and backend/app/services/github_service.py:79,97,114,139,150,152,174,176 — Dozens of print() calls instead of logger. Log output is invisible in production deployments.

items.title: No tests for useAiChat composable
items.description: frontend/src/composables/useAiChat.ts — Core chat composable handling SSE streaming, session management, and conversation state has no test coverage. Behavior regressions go undetected.

items.title: No Vue error boundary components
items.description: frontend/src/ — No error boundary components to catch and display Vue rendering errors. SSE streaming errors in chat and execution log viewers fail silently with no user feedback.

items.title: No tests for webhook payload placeholder substitution
items.description: backend/tests/: there are no tests covering ExecutionService.run_trigger() placeholder replacement logic ({paths}, {message}, {pr_url}, etc.), a critical path that determines what prompt is sent to the AI.

items.title: No tests for webhook HMAC validation edge cases
items.description: backend/tests/: webhook HMAC validation lacks tests for malformed signatures, missing headers, timing-safe comparison, and replay attacks. Only happy-path webhook delivery is tested.

items.title: No tests for concurrent rate-limit retry scheduling
items.description: backend/tests/: ExecutionService.schedule_retry() and the rate-limit backoff logic (lines 98-164) have no test coverage, leaving race conditions and retry-loop bugs undetected.

items.title: Placeholder substitution list undocumented
items.description: backend/app/services/execution_service.py lines 450-481: _KNOWN_PLACEHOLDERS set is hardcoded with no documentation on what each placeholder resolves to, how to add new ones, or where they are populated from in webhook payloads.

items.title: provider_usage_client.py (626 lines) has zero tests
items.description: backend/app/services/provider_usage_client.py: token usage tracking across providers has no dedicated test file despite being critical to budget enforcement. Add unit tests for all provider adapters and aggregation logic.

items.title: project_session_manager.py (725 lines) has zero tests
items.description: backend/app/services/project_session_manager.py: complex session state machine with no test coverage. Session state transitions, concurrent access, and timeout logic are entirely unverified.

items.title: ExecutionService tested only for token usage, not core flow
items.description: backend/tests/: only 3 tests cover ExecutionService (token extraction, no-usage-data, budget precheck). The main run_trigger() path, rate-limit retry logic, restore_pending_retries(), and error branches have no tests.

items.title: useAiChat.ts composable has no frontend tests
items.description: frontend/src/composables/useAiChat.ts: chat streaming composable — the most user-visible real-time feature — has no Vitest tests covering SSE parsing, reconnection, error states, or message accumulation.

items.title: team_monitor_service.py (220 lines) has no tests
items.description: backend/app/services/team_monitor_service.py: extensive exception handling paths, monitor lifecycle, and state transitions are all untested. Exception handling bugs here would silently degrade team monitoring.

items.title: Rate-limit retry restoration on restart is untested
items.description: backend/app/services/execution_service.py lines 214-295: restore_pending_retries() is called on startup to reschedule pending retries after a crash, but there are no tests verifying that retries are correctly persisted, loaded, and rescheduled.

items.title: Migration uses f-string for table names in SQL
items.description: backend/app/db/migrations.py lines 344-349: f"INSERT INTO {table_name}_new SELECT * FROM {table_name}" uses dynamic table names from internal code, but this pattern is fragile and sets a bad precedent. Should validate table_name against an allowlist before constructing SQL.

items.title: mcp_sync_service.py has no test coverage
items.description: backend/app/services/mcp_sync_service.py: MCP server synchronization logic is untested. Sync failures and partial-sync states could silently leave MCP configuration inconsistent.

items.title: skill_discovery_service.py has no tests
items.description: backend/app/services/skill_discovery_service.py: skill file parsing, discovery, and registration logic has no test coverage. Malformed skill files or filesystem edge cases are unverified.

items.title: Core conversation services have no unit tests
items.description: Critical services like backend/app/services/agent_conversation_service.py, backend/app/services/base_conversation_service.py, backend/app/services/session_collection_service.py have no corresponding test files. These are on the critical execution path.

items.title: Database CRUD operations lack test coverage
items.description: backend/app/db/triggers.py (1109 lines) and backend/app/db/workflows.py have complex CRUD logic but minimal test coverage. backend/tests/ has no dedicated tests for these modules, risking silent data corruption.

items.title: Most Vue components have no tests
items.description: frontend/src/ has 100+ Vue components but only ~29 test files. Workflow canvas components, monitoring charts, and most route-level views have no tests. Visual regressions and logic errors go undetected.

items.title: Audit logging not verified for all destructive operations
items.description: backend/app/services/audit_log_service.py exists but there is no mechanism to verify every `delete_*` function in backend/app/db/ calls it. Destructive operations may go unaudited, leaving no trace of who deleted what.

items.title: No tests for rate-limit retry scheduling
items.description: ExecutionService.schedule_retry() and restore_pending_retries() are complex state-management functions with closure-based timers and DB persistence but have no dedicated tests. Need tests for exponential backoff, timer cancellation, thread safety, and DB round-trips.

items.title: Workflow node error modes lack targeted tests
items.description: Workflow node error modes (stop, continue, continue_with_error) each have distinct branching paths in WorkflowExecutionService, but test coverage for edge cases (node timeout + continue_with_error, cycle detection + partial execution) is missing.

items.title: Insufficient request body validation in routes
items.description: Route handlers in backend/app/routes/ accept request.get_json() but validate fields minimally before passing to DB. No schema validation for nested objects, array bounds, or type coercion. Should use Pydantic models for all request bodies.

items.title: Background services not initialized in tests
items.description: backend/app/__init__.py spawns SchedulerService, MonitoringService, RotationEvaluator, CLIProxyManager only in non-testing mode. Their behavior is untested in conftest.py, creating gaps in integration coverage.

items.title: No tests for orchestration_service.py
items.description: backend/app/services/orchestration_service.py (~290 lines) has no dedicated test file. Critical paths like all-backends-rate-limited fallback, account selection between specific and auto-pick modes, and budget-check-triggered retry scheduling are completely untested.

items.title: GitHub webhook tests missing critical edge cases
items.description: backend/tests/test_github_webhook.py covers basic scenarios but is missing: HMAC signature verification failures, malformed PR data, per-repo rate limiting under concurrent events, and retry scheduling behavior.

items.title: Token extraction in budget_service is untested
items.description: backend/app/services/budget_service.py lines 92-114 uses fragile regex + JSON scanning for token parsing. No tests for mixed stdout/stderr, timeout scenarios, or truncated output — wrong token counts lead to incorrect budget tracking.

items.title: SSE backoff and reconnect logic lacks tests
items.description: frontend/src/services/api/client.ts createBackoffEventSource() implements exponential backoff with jitter but has no tests for: backpressure queue overflow, event loss during reconnection, or jitter calculation correctness.

items.title: orchestration_service.py has no dedicated unit tests
items.description: backend/app/services/orchestration_service.py contains fallback chain logic and execution dispatch but has no test file. The validate_fallback_chain_entries() logic is especially untested.

items.title: process_manager.py has no tests
items.description: backend/app/services/process_manager.py handles process tracking and cancellation but has no test file covering register/unregister/cancel flows or the race condition noted above.

items.title: rate_limit_service.py has no unit tests
items.description: backend/app/services/rate_limit_service.py manages rate limit state but has no dedicated test file. Rate limit edge cases (boundary hits, reset timing) are untested.

items.title: Core frontend composables have zero test coverage
items.description: frontend/src/composables/useConversation.ts, useAiChat.ts, useTeamCanvas.ts, and useOrgCanvas.ts have no test files. These contain complex logic (SSE handling, topology detection) that would benefit from unit tests.

items.title: Critical execution paths in execution_service.py lack unit tests
items.description: backend/app/services/execution_service.py: GitHub cloning failures, prompt template placeholder resolution, and stream handling edge cases (timeout, partial reads) are not unit-tested — only covered by broader integration tests.

items.title: types.ts has 1567 lines mixing 15+ entity domains
items.description: frontend/src/services/api/types.ts is 1567 lines with 50+ types for agents, conversations, teams, skills, hooks, etc. all in one file. Split into domain-scoped files (types/agent.ts, types/team.ts, etc.) for maintainability.

items.title: DB migration exceptions silently caught
items.description: backend/app/db/migrations.py lines 2625, 2636: Two bare except Exception catches during schema migration silently log errors and continue, potentially leaving the database in a partially-migrated state without aborting.

items.title: No test coverage for pty_service and cliproxy_manager
items.description: backend/app/services/pty_service.py and cliproxy_manager.py contain process lifecycle management logic with no dedicated tests. Process startup, health check, and cleanup failure paths are completely untested, making regressions in CLI execution invisible.

items.title: No tests for cycle detection in useWorkflowValidation
items.description: frontend/src/composables/useWorkflowValidation.ts implements DFS-based cycle detection and port type compatibility matching. Neither algorithm has test coverage. Incorrect cycle detection could silently allow invalid workflow graphs that hang on execution.

items.title: Composable error paths have no test coverage
items.description: frontend/src/composables/useAiChat.ts, useConversation.ts, and useProjectSession.ts have minimal tests that only cover happy paths. SSE reconnection, malformed event handling, session creation failure, and stream error events are all untested despite being critical user-facing failure modes.

items.title: Untyped any[] props in canvas components
items.description: frontend/src/components/canvas/TeamCanvas.vue, OrgCanvas.vue, CanvasSidebar.vue, AgentDetailPanel.vue, and ProjectTeamCanvas.vue all declare props as `members: any[]`, `availableAgents: any[]`, `availableSuperAgents: any[]`. This disables type checking for the entire member/agent rendering pipeline.

items.title: Untyped any[] in monitoring chart data structures
items.description: frontend/src/components/monitoring/CombinedUsageChart.vue, TokenUsageChart.vue, RotationTimelineChart.vue, and RemainingTimeChart.vue all use `const datasets: any[]` or `Record<number, any[]>` for chart data. Chart.js has full TypeScript types available that would catch data shape mismatches at compile time.

items.title: Broad `except Exception` in route handlers without specific types
items.description: backend/app/routes/plugin_exports.py has 8+ instances of bare `except Exception as e:`. backend/app/__init__.py lines 150-236 uses multiple broad `except Exception` blocks in app initialization. Specific exceptions (sqlite3.Error, ValueError, KeyError) should be caught individually to avoid masking unexpected errors.

items.title: Router guards indistinguishably swallow all errors
items.description: frontend/src/router/guards.ts lines 24-58: All 12 entity validators wrap API calls in try/catch returning false on any error. A 404 (entity doesn't exist) is treated the same as a network timeout or 500, leading to confusing redirects instead of proper error pages.

items.title: 15+ empty catch blocks in composables
items.description: frontend/src/composables/useProjectSession.ts lines 55, 71, 96 and throughout: over 15 catch (e) {} blocks with no logging or user feedback. Silent failures make client-side debugging very difficult.

items.title: New audit_log_service has no tests
items.description: backend/app/services/audit_log_service.py: Recently added service with no test file verifying JSON format, field redaction, or integration with the logging pipeline. Any regression would go undetected.

items.title: No automated migration correctness tests
items.description: backend/app/db/migrations.py: No tests verify that migrations run cleanly, produce the expected schema, or that rollback strategy works. A regression in migration logic could corrupt production data.

items.title: API client retry/error logic untested
items.description: frontend/src/services/api/client.ts: The sophisticated retry logic (exponential backoff, jitter, 429 handling, SSE backpressure) has no unit tests. Coverage gaps in the network layer mean regressions will only be caught in production.

items.title: Unknown prompt template placeholders silently pass
items.description: backend/app/services/execution_service.py lines 625-645: Warns about unknown placeholders but continues executing. A malformed template with bad placeholders produces a prompt with literal {placeholder} text instead of failing fast with a clear error.

items.title: SSE event JSON not schema-validated before processing
items.description: frontend/src/composables/useConversation.ts around line 40: SSE stream parser processes server-sent JSON events without validating they match expected schema. Invalid events are silently skipped, hiding bugs in the event serialization layer.

items.title: List endpoints return unbounded results
items.description: backend/app/routes/: Endpoints like list_agents, list_bots, list_teams return all results without pagination. Organizations with hundreds of entities will experience degraded performance and large memory spikes.

items.title: Visual DAG Workflow Builder
items.description: A drag-and-drop canvas to chain bots and agents into multi-step pipelines with conditional branches and parallel execution paths. Solves the pain of manually wiring bots together via prompts — teams can visually compose 'PR review → security scan → changelog update' into a single reusable workflow.

items.title: Execution Replay & Re-run
items.description: Replay any past execution with the exact same prompt, context, and inputs — or modify just the prompt and re-run. Solves the pain of debugging bot failures where you need to reproduce the exact conditions without waiting for the next webhook event.

items.title: Prompt Version History
items.description: Track every change to bot prompt templates with diffs, authored timestamps, and one-click rollback. Teams break prompt templates and have no way to recover the last working version — this adds Git-like history to prompts without requiring any Git knowledge.

items.title: Multi-Provider Fallback Chains
items.description: Configure ordered fallback chains (e.g., Claude → OpenCode → Gemini) per bot so that if one provider fails or rate-limits, execution automatically retries on the next. Eliminates the single point of failure that causes automation blackouts during provider outages.

items.title: Bot Template Marketplace
items.description: A curated gallery of pre-built bot templates (PR reviewer, release note generator, dependency auditor, standup summarizer) that teams can install with one click and customize. Dramatically reduces time-to-value for new teams who don't know what automations to start with.

items.title: AI Cost & Token Usage Dashboard
items.description: Track token consumption, estimated API cost, and execution time per bot, team, and project over time with trend charts. Engineering managers have no visibility into AI spend — this surfaces cost attribution before bills arrive.

items.title: Slack Execution Notifications
items.description: Route bot execution results, failures, and summaries to configurable Slack channels with message templates. Teams miss critical failures because they have to actively check the dashboard — push notifications eliminate the monitoring burden.

items.title: Human-in-the-Loop Approval Gates
items.description: Add optional approval steps to workflows where a designated team member must approve the AI output before it's posted, merged, or acted upon. Addresses the trust problem — teams want AI automation but not fully autonomous actions on production systems.

items.title: In-Platform Prompt Playground
items.description: Test and iterate on bot prompts directly in the dashboard with a live preview panel, sample inputs, and output history — without triggering a real execution. Engineers waste time deploying prompt changes just to see if they work; this tightens the iteration loop dramatically.

items.title: Execution Digest Reports
items.description: Daily or weekly email/Slack digests summarizing all bot executions: what ran, what succeeded, what failed, and cost summaries. Managers currently have no async way to stay informed — digests deliver situational awareness without requiring dashboard visits.

items.title: Secrets & Credential Vault
items.description: A built-in encrypted secrets store for API keys, tokens, and environment variables that bots can reference by name in prompts and CLI configs. Teams currently hardcode credentials or manage them out-of-band — centralizing secrets improves security and reduces onboarding friction.

items.title: Conditional Trigger Rules Engine
items.description: Add filter conditions to triggers (e.g., 'only run PR Review bot if PR has >200 lines changed' or 'only on files matching src/**') using a simple rule builder. Bots run too broadly today, wasting tokens and adding noise — conditions let teams target automation precisely.

items.title: Execution Log Annotations
items.description: Allow engineers to annotate specific log lines with notes, flag them as bugs or insights, and link to follow-up issues. Execution logs are ephemeral and hard to mine — annotations turn raw output into institutional knowledge.

items.title: Agent Quality Scoring
items.description: Collect thumbs-up/thumbs-down feedback on bot outputs and surface per-agent quality scores over time with trend analysis. Teams have no quantitative way to know if their bots are getting better or worse as they iterate on prompts.

items.title: Issue Tracker Integration (Jira / Linear)
items.description: Create triggers from Jira/Linear issue events (new bug, status change, label added) and allow bots to write back comments or update issue fields. Extends automation beyond GitHub to the full engineering workflow where work is actually tracked.

items.title: Execution Rate Limits & Quotas
items.description: Set per-bot, per-team, or per-project daily/hourly execution caps with alerting when approaching limits. Without guardrails, a misconfigured trigger can run a bot thousands of times and blow through API budgets overnight.

items.title: Team Activity Feed
items.description: A real-time chronological feed showing all bot runs, configuration changes, and team member actions across a project — like a GitHub activity timeline but for automation. Removes the 'who ran what and when' mystery that slows incident response.

items.title: One-Click Bot Cloning
items.description: Clone any existing bot (including its triggers, prompt template, and settings) as a starting point for a new one. Teams repeatedly build similar bots from scratch — cloning compresses the creation of variant automations from hours to seconds.

items.title: Context File Attachments for Bots
items.description: Attach reference files (coding standards, architecture docs, runbooks) to a bot so the AI always has project-specific context injected into its prompt. Without this, teams bloat prompts with static context or get generic AI outputs that ignore their conventions.

items.title: Execution Dependency Graph
items.description: Visualize which bots triggered other bots or workflows in a given execution chain, with timing and status overlaid. Complex pipelines become black boxes — the graph makes causal relationships and bottlenecks immediately visible.

items.title: Role-Based Access Control (RBAC)
items.description: Fine-grained permissions so team admins can restrict who can create bots, trigger executions, view logs, or manage credentials. As teams grow, unrestricted access becomes a liability — RBAC aligns the platform with enterprise security requirements.

items.title: GitHub App Installation Flow
items.description: Replace manual webhook setup with a first-class GitHub App installation that auto-configures repositories, permissions, and event subscriptions. Manual webhook setup is the #1 onboarding drop-off point — removing it gets teams to first automation in minutes.

items.title: Structured Output Action Blocks
items.description: Let bots emit structured action blocks (create PR comment, open GitHub issue, post Slack message, update file) that the platform executes automatically — rather than the bot doing it via CLI. Separates AI reasoning from side-effect execution, making outputs auditable and reversible.

items.title: Dry-Run Mode
items.description: Execute a bot against real inputs but suppress all external side effects (no PR comments posted, no files changed) and display what would have happened. Teams fear running new bots in production — dry-run eliminates the risk of first execution.

items.title: AI-Powered Trigger Suggestions
items.description: Analyze a repository's GitHub events and activity patterns to suggest relevant trigger configurations and starter prompt templates. New users stare at a blank configuration screen — contextual suggestions reduce time-to-first-bot from days to minutes.

items.title: Full-Text Execution Log Search
items.description: Search across all historical execution logs by keyword, bot name, date range, or status. Currently logs are only viewable per-execution — search transforms the execution history into a queryable knowledge base for debugging and auditing.

items.title: Incoming Webhook Inspector
items.description: Capture and display raw incoming webhook payloads in a real-time inspector panel, showing which bots matched, which were skipped, and why. Debugging trigger mismatches is currently impossible without external tooling — the inspector makes it self-service.

items.title: Programmatic API Keys
items.description: Generate scoped API keys so teams can trigger bots and query execution results from CI pipelines, scripts, or other tools. The platform is currently web-only — API keys unlock it as a programmable automation primitive embedded in any workflow.

items.title: Auto-Generated Changelogs
items.description: A dedicated bot template that aggregates merged PRs, AI summaries, and linked issues into a formatted CHANGELOG.md on each release tag. Writing changelogs is universally hated and perpetually delayed — this eliminates the task entirely.

items.title: Guided Onboarding Wizard
items.description: A step-by-step first-run experience that walks new teams through connecting a repo, choosing a starter bot, and firing their first test execution — with inline guidance at each step. Blank-slate onboarding is the biggest conversion killer; this removes every ambiguity.
